questions.question: Define the hypothesis function, cost function, and gradient descent update rule for simple linear regression with one variable.
keyPoints: Gradient descent: θj := θj - α * (1/m) Σ (hθ(x^(i)) - y^(i)) xj^(i)
α is the learning rate; update simultaneously for θ0 and θ1

questions.question: Explain the normal equation for computing the optimal parameters in closed form. When is it preferable over gradient descent?
keyPoints: Normal equation: θ = (X^T X)^(-1) X^T y
Eliminates need for iterative updates and learning rate
Preferable when number of features (n) is small (<10,000) and matrix X^T X is invertible
Drawbacks: O(n^3) computational complexity and numerical instability if X^T X is singular

questions.question: Discuss the key assumptions of simple linear regression. How can you validate each assumption using diagnostic plots and statistical tests? Provide a practical example of a one-variable regression problem and outline the steps you would take to build, evaluate, and interpret the model.
keyPoints: Assumptions: linearity, independence, homoscedasticity, normality of residuals, no perfect multicollinearity (only one predictor here)
Diagnostic tools: scatter plot (linearity), residual vs fitted (homoscedasticity), Q‑Q plot (normality), Durbin‑Watson test (independence)

Practical example: predicting house price from floor area. Steps: data collection, exploratory analysis, fit model using gradient descent or normal equation, evaluate with MSE, RMSE, MAE, R², interpret coefficient θ1 (price per sq ft) and intercept
Model validation: train‑test split, cross‑validation, residual analysis, checking for outliers, ensuring assumption satisfaction before using model for decision making

questions.question: Explain the R‑squared (R²) metric for evaluating a linear regression model. How is it computed, what does a value close to 1 indicate, and what are its limitations? Additionally, propose an alternative evaluation metric and justify its use.
keyPoints: R² = 1 - SS_res / SS_tot, where SS_res = Σ (y_i - ŷ_i)^2 and SS_tot = Σ (y_i - ȳ)^2
Values near 1 imply the model explains most of the variance; values near 0 indicate poor fit
Limitations: can be misleading for non‑linear data, can increase artificially with added predictors (adjusted R² addresses this), not a measure of prediction accuracy
Alternative metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) or Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) – more robust to outliers and directly interpretable in the same units as the target variable
